paper_title: Sediment Core Analysis and Pollen Diversity Methods

methods.method_name: Sediment Core Collection and Charcoal Analysis
methods.description: This method describes the process for collecting a sediment core from Qinghai Lake, sectioning it, and subsequently extracting and counting macroscopic charcoal particles from the sections for analysis.

steps.description: A 832 cm long sediment core (TCQH1) was extracted in waters 6.3 m deep near the centre of Qinghai Lake, in November 2010 using a UWITEC piston corer.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment core using a piston corer from a specified location and depth.

steps.description: The core was sectioned at 1 cm intervals.
steps.searchable_summary: Section sediment core into discrete samples at regular intervals.

steps.description: Samples were stored at 4 • C until analysed.
steps.searchable_summary: Store sediment samples under controlled temperature conditions.

steps.description: Macroscopic charcoal particles (> 125 µm in diameter) were extracted from 1 cm 3 samples at contiguous 1 cm intervals.
steps.searchable_summary: Take sub-samples (1 cm³) from core sections for charcoal analysis.

steps.description: Charcoal samples were soaked in 20 mL of 5 % sodium hexametaphosphate for > 24 h
steps.searchable_summary: Soak sediment samples in sodium hexametaphosphate to disaggregate sediment.

steps.description: and 20 mL of 10 % H 2 O 2 solution for 24 h to disaggregate the sediment (Huerta et al., 2009).
steps.searchable_summary: Soak sediment samples in hydrogen peroxide to further disaggregate organic matter.

steps.description: Samples were gently washed through a 125 µm mesh sieve
steps.searchable_summary: Wash and sieve sediment samples to isolate particles larger than 125 µm.

steps.description: and the residue was transferred into gridded Petri dishes
steps.searchable_summary: Transfer sieved residue to gridded Petri dishes for counting.

steps.description: and counted under a stereomicroscope at magnifications of 40×.
steps.searchable_summary: Count macroscopic charcoal particles in the residue using a stereomicroscope.
outputs: Macroscopic charcoal particle counts per sample

methods.description: This method describes how the age-depth relationship for the sediment core was established using a combination of radiometric and radiocarbon dating methods and a Bayesian modeling approach, and how sedimentation rates were calculated.

steps.description: The age-depth model for the core has been established in the previously published study (Xiao et al., 2015), based on the 210 Pb and 137 Cs dates
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain existing 210Pb and 137Cs dating data for the sediment core.
inputs: Sediment core (implicitly, as dates are for this core)

steps.description: and the AMS 14 C dates
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain existing AMS 14C dating data for the sediment core.

steps.description: and using the recently developed Bayesian method (Blaauw and Christen, 2011).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply a Bayesian method to combine dating data and establish an age-depth model.

steps.description: According to the model, the bottom date is presumed to be 18.5 ka
steps.searchable_summary: Determine the age of the bottom of the core based on the established age-depth model.

steps.description: and the average temporal sampling resolution is ∼ 22 years for the macroscopic charcoal record.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the average temporal resolution represented by each sample interval using the age-depth model.

steps.description: The sedimentation rate before ∼ 0.9 ka is relatively low and steady, with a mean of 0.0385 cm a −1 .Between ∼ 0.9 ka and AD 1952, the rate is evidently higher at 0.124 cm a −1 .After AD 1952, the rate is far higher than the earlier periods, with a mean of 0.741 cm a −1 (Fig. 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate sedimentation rates for different time periods based on the age-depth model.

methods.description: This method outlines the procedures used to calculate palynological richness and Simpson's reciprocal index from pollen count data to evaluate plant diversity.

steps.description: Palynological richness index is the number of different pollen types in every pollen sample.
steps.searchable_summary: Define palynological richness as the count of unique pollen types per sample.

steps.description: Because pollen counts in samples may differ, rarefaction analysis was used to estimate palynological richness (Birks and Line, 1992), which was computed using the "vegan" package for R (http://CRAN.R-project.org/package=vegan)(Oksanen et al., 2016).
steps.searchable_summary: Estimate palynological richness using rarefaction analysis, accounting for varying sample sizes.
inputs: Pollen count data (number of individuals per pollen type per sample)
outputs: Rarefied palynological richness values per sample

steps.description: Simpson's reciprocal index (1/D) was calculated as where n i is the number of individuals in the ith pollen types, N the total number of individuals, and S the total number of pollen types (Xiao et al., 2008).The higher values of this index indicate the greater sample evenness.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate Simpson's reciprocal index (1/D) for each sample using the provided formula.
inputs: Pollen count data (n_i: individuals per type, N: total individuals, S: total types) for each sample
outputs: Simpson's reciprocal index values per sample

methods.description: This method describes the process of standardizing pollen percentage data using min-max normalization to facilitate visualization and comparison among different pollen types.

steps.description: In this study, we use min-max normalization to standardize pollen percentages, which can eliminate the influence of their values and better visualize the interrelation among percentage variations of the selected pollen types in the same panel of one figure.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply min-max normalization to standardize pollen percentage data for selected types.
inputs: Pollen percentage data for selected pollen types

steps.description: The formula is x * = x i −min max−min , where x * is the standardized data of the selected pollen type, x i is percentage of this pollen type in the ith sample, max is the maximum value of this pollen type percentage, and min is the minimum value of this pollen type percentage.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply the min-max scaling formula: x* = (x_i - min) / (max - min).
inputs: Original pollen percentage (x_i) for a sample
Minimum percentage value (min) for the specific pollen type across all samples
Maximum percentage value (max) for the specific pollen type across all samples
